identity.title: Produce archive-store selection and failure evidence

authorization.reference: Operator authorized Phase 0 planning, requested WP-FF-008 through WP-FF-011 as detailed no-context STUB packets linked to the canonical design and readiness topics, and required the stubs to be committed and pushed before WP-FF-003 implementation begins.

blockers: WP-FF-003 executable gate bootstrap must be VALIDATED before this compiled spike starts.
The exact pure-Rust candidate set and current source-level research are not yet recorded.
Archive identity and commit/archive contracts from WP-FF-005 and WP-FF-009 must be stable enough to define equal workloads and failure oracles.
lifecycle.next_action: After prerequisite packets are proven, refresh current archive-store research, select maintained pure-Rust candidates without authorizing SQLite, promote this STUB to READY_FOR_DEV, and execute the crash/concurrency/migration/performance evidence matrix.

scope.summary: Resolve the archive-backend readiness gate with a bounded Rust-first spike that measures a maintained pure-Rust store against the complete archive contract and records an evidence matrix; SQLite remains unapproved unless the Operator later accepts a narrow dependency exception based on the same evidence.
in_scope: Define separate item, representation, track, asset, and derived-output identities and the uniqueness/provenance keys the archive must persist.
Implement or adapt the smallest prototype archive adapter needed to test transactional uniqueness, concurrent claims, leases, successful-output insertion, and idempotent reconciliation.
Exercise crash recovery at every archive-relevant durable prefix, including output-without-row, row-without-output, stale lease, torn record, duplicate claim, and interrupted migration.
Measure membership lookup, insertion, write amplification, memory, storage size, and recovery behavior at representative and 1,000,000-identity scales.
Test schema creation, forward migration, interrupted migration, rollback or last-known-good recovery, import of mapped yt-dlp text archives, and explicit rejection of unknown identity mappings.
Produce a machine-readable candidate evidence matrix and decision-ready result that distinguishes PASS, FAIL, unsupported capability, and residual uncertainty.
Return failed-candidate evidence to the Operator without silently adding SQLite, a remote database, native bindings, or another runtime dependency.
non_goals: Authorizing SQLite, PostgreSQL, a remote database, native bindings, or another runtime dependency.
Implementing the complete production storage subsystem, all output sinks, or all archive migration formats.
Claiming complete replacement readiness, parity, or production durability from a prototype-only result.
Changing extractor, protocol, collection, or final product scope to make a candidate pass.
Treating benchmark speed as acceptance when crash, identity, lease, migration, or reconciliation correctness fails.
dependencies: WP-FF-005-versioned-core-contracts-v1 accepted archive identity and state-machine inputs
WP-FF-009-resource-durability-models-v1 accepted durable-prefix, filesystem-capability, and reconciliation models
Pinned yt-dlp 2026.07.04 archive oracle cases from WP-FF-004
Canonical architecture, PR, and triggered deep gates implemented by fforager-xtask

acceptance_matrix.requirement: A versioned machine-readable archive contract defines identity levels, uniqueness keys, provenance, success-event insertion, leases, reconciliation, migrations, import mapping, supported filesystem guarantees, limits, and error outcomes without assuming a storage engine.

At least one maintained pure-Rust candidate is inspected at source/configuration/test level and exercised through the same declared archive workload; every rejected candidate has a concrete capability, maintenance, dependency, or proof failure.

Deterministic fault tests cover concurrent duplicate claims, stale leases, crashes at every archive/commit prefix, torn or invalid records, output-row mismatch, restart reconciliation, and interrupted migration, and produce no false success or silently accepted inconsistency.

The candidate evidence report includes crash, concurrency, migration, lease, reconciliation, filesystem, latency, RSS, storage-size, and write-amplification results for declared corpus sizes, including benchmark-matrix row B-021, with exact command, timeout, fixture hash, environment, and failure oracle.

The result selects a pure-Rust backend only if every mandatory correctness gate passes; otherwise it produces Operator-ready exception evidence and leaves SQLite and other native/runtime dependencies unauthorized.
FF-GATE-ARCH-001, FF-GATE-PR-001, the triggered archive deep tests, and an independent adversarial review pass on the same source and evidence manifest.

checks_run: git status --porcelain=v2 --branch: main at 447b4dbb0d64df56b3c956d8f311ff24f5b51c50 before packet creation
git worktree list --porcelain: one main worktree; no packet worktree created
blockers: Packet is a STUB and does not authorize implementation.
last_checkpoint.next_action: Wait for prerequisites, then refresh research and promote the packet before creating prototype code.

not_proven: Archive crash safety or production durability

repository_layout.runtime_boundary: Shipped product execution must require neither .GOV/ nor build/.

promotion_requirements: Prerequisite packet evidence is available and source-compatible.
Current broad research is recorded with source dates, inspected implementation evidence, candidate maintenance status, rejected options, and validation plan.
The exact candidate dependency graph and licenses pass policy preflight.
Acceptance commands, fixture versions, report schema, timeouts, and failure oracles are frozen.
The Operator is asked only if evidence supports a specific dependency-boundary exception.

documentation_topics.heading: Fixed dependency and readiness boundary
documentation_topics.use: Rust/FFmpeg boundary; archive backend undecided pending pure-Rust spike

documentation_topics.heading: Archive-backed duplicate prevention
documentation_topics.use: archive behavior, bounded actor, lease/reconciliation, and evidence requirements

documentation_topics.heading: Journal, checkpoints, commit, reconciliation, and filesystem capability

readiness_matrix_items.required_resolution: Run a Rust-only storage spike; request a specific exception only if measured evidence proves it necessary.
Crash-safe staged commit, path, filesystem capability, and startup reconciliation.
Benchmark and fault-test a maintained pure-Rust candidate first.
SQLite unapproved; separate identity levels and archive only reconcilable committed outputs.

readiness_matrix_items.gate: Archive store crash, concurrency, migration, lease, and reconciliation result
Commit/archive/resume fault injection across every durable state prefix

readiness_matrix_items.output: Pure-Rust archive-store versus SQLite evidence matrix without pre-approving SQLite

base_scope: Complete mandatory correctness and fault matrix

high_roi_additions.addition: Use one reusable deterministic fault-schedule and report schema for archive, journal, and later update-activation tests.
high_roi_additions.why_high_roi: The durable-prefix harness is already required and avoids rebuilding fault orchestration for later packets.
high_roi_additions.gap_closed: Crash claims otherwise depend on ad hoc tests.
high_roi_additions.validation: Mutation fixture must remove a crash prefix and make the deep gate fail.

high_roi_additions.addition: Emit explain-identity and explain-reconciliation records with stable IDs in every test case.
high_roi_additions.why_high_roi: Identity diagnostics are cheap while the schema is being defined and make operator/model debugging deterministic.
high_roi_additions.gap_closed: Archive skips or duplicates can otherwise be opaque.
high_roi_additions.validation: Golden reports cover distinct, duplicate, migrated, and inconsistent identities.

high_roi_additions.addition: Retain candidate-neutral workload and result adapters so a future Operator-approved backend can run the exact same matrix.
high_roi_additions.why_high_roi: It prevents evidence drift and makes later dependency adjudication cheaper.
high_roi_additions.gap_closed: Different backends could otherwise be judged on unequal tests.
high_roi_additions.validation: A fake failing backend proves the harness detects contract violations without backend-specific assertions.

reused_systems: WP-FF-003 locked workspace and executable gates
gaps_closed: Unproven identity separation and uniqueness semantics
SQLite dependency temptation without measured need

red_team.risk: A candidate appears fast but loses or duplicates claims after a crash.
failure_scenarios: power loss after output rename before archive row
mitigations: never admit speed samples from failed correctness runs
red_team.verification: Seeded crash matrix plus restart oracle; mutated missing-prefix fixture must fail.

red_team.risk: Archive identity is too broad or unstable.
failure_scenarios: same asset redownloads after schema upgrade
red_team.verification: Collision/alias corpus and backwards/forwards migration checks.

failure_scenarios: row exists but output is missing
red_team.verification: Every mismatch is injected and produces the declared recovery or inconsistency result.

red_team.risk: A native dependency is introduced implicitly through a transitive Cargo link.
mitigations: explicit Operator decision only after evidence
red_team.verification: Negative dependency fixtures fail architecture and dependency policy gates.

red_team.risk: Large-corpus tests pass by loading all identities into memory.
failure_scenarios: cache makes reported lookup unrealistically cheap
red_team.verification: B-021 profiles enforce declared memory ceiling and cache state.

required_proof_classes: JSON schema and golden contract fixtures
fault injection across every durable prefix
verification_needs.closure_rule: No PASS if any mandatory correctness row fails, evidence comes from a different source/fixture manifest, or the selected backend requires an unapproved runtime/native dependency.
